prompt:
The monitoring vendor can push events to us over plain HTTP with an HMAC header, and I'd rather receive them with a small PowerShell listener on the ops box than stand up a whole web stack. Ticket: `hookspool.ps1`, a webhook receiver built on `[System.Net.HttpListener]`.

Parameters: `-Port <int>`, `-Secret <string>`, `-Count <int>`, `-SpoolPath <file>`, `-ManifestPath <file>`, `-ReadyPath <file>`.

Behavior:
- Listen on `http://127.0.0.1:<Port>/`. Once the listener is actually started, create the ready file (any content) — the deploy tooling waits on it before telling the vendor to send.
- Handle exactly `-Count` requests, then write the manifest and exit 0.
- `POST /hooks` carries a JSON event and a header `X-Hook-Signature: sha256=<hex>`, the HMAC-SHA256 of the RAW request body bytes keyed with the shared secret (UTF-8). Verify with .NET (`[System.Security.Cryptography.HMACSHA256]`). Hex comparison is case-insensitive — the vendor's staging sender uppercases, that's documented and fine. Valid: respond 200 and append the body, byte-for-byte as received, plus a newline to the spool file (UTF-8, NO BOM — NDJSON, one event per line). Invalid: respond 401 and spool nothing; the manifest records the reason: `missing-signature` when the header is absent, `bad-signature` when it doesn't verify.
- Non-POST on /hooks → 405; anything on another path → 404. These count as handled requests (`other` in the manifest) but never touch the spool.
- The manifest is ONE line of compact JSON, keys in exactly this order: `received` (all handled requests), `accepted`, `rejected`, `other`, `acceptedIds` (the `id` field of each accepted event, arrival order), `rejectedReasons` (arrival order). No trailing newline.

Event bodies can contain non-ASCII (the Montréal site loves accents) — sign-check and spool must work on bytes, not on re-encoded strings.

Verify with `pwsh -NoProfile -File test_hookspool.ps1`: it starts your receiver, fires a scripted series of good, unsigned, wrongly-signed and wrong-path requests at it from a Python sender, and pins the status codes, the exact spool bytes, and the manifest. The test file and send_hooks.py stay untouched.
notes: 6P6 item 6: the one 6P6 seed where pwsh is the SERVER (HttpListener probed working on Linux pwsh 7.6.3, 2026-07-16) and the python script is the sender, per catalog. HMAC-SHA256 over raw body bytes with a shared-secret fixture; scripted sender covers valid, wrong-secret, missing-header, stale-signature (signature of a different body), uppercase-hex-accepted, wrong-method (405), wrong-path (404), and a trailing valid event; harness pins sender-observed status codes, spool bytes (UTF-8 no BOM, non-ASCII event pins raw-byte handling), and the accept/reject manifest. Receiver lifecycle (ready-file after listener start, exit after -Count) is what makes the loopback dance deterministic. Vocabulary kept mundane per 2026-07-13 note (staging sender quirks, not adversaries). Distinct from py-webhook-verify (pure signature-verification lib, py, no server) and py-hookrelay (relay/forwarding pipeline): this is a live HTTP receiver with spool+manifest contract. catalog wave6/6P6/6.
